summary: Khailo argues that choosing C++ over Java for computationally intensive projects is not just a technical decision but a moral one, since C++ offers better performance per watt and thus lower energy consumption. He critiques Silicon Valley's cognitive dissonance in promising a utopian tech future while ignoring the realities of climate change and resource consumption.
key_points: C++ offers superior performance per watt compared to Java, requiring less hardware and energy for computationally massive workloads
Silicon Valley's promised future of technological abundance conflicts with climate science's warnings about resource limits
The software industry suffers from cognitive dissonance, ignoring the environmental impact of inefficient code
Programming language choice has real-world energy and environmental consequences at scale
money_quotes: My argument to him to keep it C++ was not just technical but moral. I told him 'C++ will use less energy. Performance per Watt is higher. We will need less hardware.'
Somehow the promised future of Silicon Valley and the disaster of climate change is not in conflict in peoples minds.
The worst of it is that the software culture is completely ignoring this cognitive dissonance.

I know it may sound stupid, but using C++ in this case, for me, was a conscious moral decision, not just a technical one. Because if we want anything like the future the Valley is promising us, we need to start using less now.
key_insight: Programming language choices at scale are moral decisions with real environmental consequences, and the tech industry's refusal to reckon with this reflects a deeper cognitive dissonance between its utopian promises and ecological reality.
